title: Welcome to Java
successMessage: Your first Java program! That boilerplate — public class Main, public static void main — will become second nature. For now, just know: it's the entry point every Java program needs.

title: Print multiple lines
instruction:
Each `System.out.println()` prints on its own line and adds a newline at the end.

`System.out.print()` prints without the newline — useful for building output piece by piece.

Add two more `println` calls inside `main`.

codeChecks.message: Add two more System.out.println() calls — three total.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Three lines, three outputs. Java runs them top to bottom. Every program you ever write follows this rule: instructions execute in sequence, inside main.

title: Comments — document your code
instruction:
Java has three comment styles:

- `// single-line` — everything after `//` on that line
- `/* multi-line */` — spans multiple lines
- `/** Javadoc */` — generates documentation (you'll use this with classes)

Comments are ignored by the compiler. Run this — notice none of the comments appear in output.
successMessage: Comments document WHY — not what. Good comments are rare and valuable. Bad comments repeat the code in English. Write code that explains itself, and comments that explain the reasoning.

title: String concatenation with +
instruction:
In Java, `+` between strings joins them together:

```java
System.out.println("Hello, " + "World");
// Hello, World
```

If one side is a number, Java converts it to a String automatically when combined with `+`.

Print your name and age using concatenation.
hint: System.out.println("My name is " + "Alex" + " and I am " + 25 + " years old.");

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: String concatenation with +. Note: "1" + 2 gives "12" — Java converts the number to a string when mixed with string concatenation. You'll see this behaviour a lot.

title: Arithmetic output
instruction:
Java handles arithmetic directly inside `System.out.println`:

```java
System.out.println(10 + 3);   // 13
System.out.println(10 - 3);   // 7
System.out.println(10 * 3);   // 30
System.out.println(10 / 3);   // 3 — integer division!
System.out.println(10 % 3);   // 1 — remainder
```

⚠️ `10 / 3` gives `3` not `3.33` — because both are integers. Use `10.0 / 3` for decimal division.

Run all six operations.
successMessage: Integer division truncates — 10/3 is 3, not 3.33. This catches everyone at least once. Whenever you need decimal precision, make at least one operand a double: 10.0/3.

title: Spot and fix the error
instruction:
Java is compiled — syntax errors are caught before the program ever runs. This one has a missing semicolon and a wrong method name.

Java requires semicolons at the end of every statement. Find and fix both bugs.
hint: All statements end with ; and the method is System.out.println (lowercase p)

codeChecks.message: Add the missing semicolon after the first println.
Fix the method name: PrintLn → println (lowercase p)

successMessage: Two bugs: missing semicolon and wrong capitalisation. Java is case-sensitive — println, not PrintLn. The compiler error messages tell you exactly what line to fix.

title: What is a class?
instruction:
In Java, **everything lives inside a class**. The class named `Main` matches the filename `Main.java`.

`public` — accessible from anywhere
`static` — belongs to the class, not an instance
`void` — returns nothing
`main` — the entry point Java looks for
`String[] args` — command-line arguments (mostly ignored for now)

You don't need to understand all of this yet — but you'll know exactly what it means by Chapter 9. For now, just recognise the structure.
successMessage: Main.class.getSimpleName() reads the class name at runtime. Every Java file has exactly one public class matching the filename. This structure will make complete sense by Chapter 9.

title: Write it from scratch
instruction:
No starter code. Write everything yourself.

Print exactly:
```
Java is compiled.
Java is typed.
Java is everywhere.
Chapter 1 complete!
```

Four `System.out.println` calls. Match the text exactly.
hint: public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(...); } }
successMessage: Built from nothing! Class, main method, four print statements — you typed it all. That structure becomes invisible muscle memory within a few chapters. Chapter 1 done!
